reply_text: ​@shredd5705it's the core structure of the Knives Out series. Benoit Blanc isn't the protagonist of these movies. With each movie there's a different protagonist that the story arc is built around, someone for the audience to root for emotionally. Benoit Blanc exists to help him/her to complete their arc. It's what gives these films an emotional journey rather than just a puzzle to be solved.

video_title: Wake Up Dead Man: A Knives Out Mystery | Official Teaser | Netflix
reply_text:
​@shredd5705 the Knives Out films technically aren't really whodunits. They don't constantly make every suspect dig themselves deeper doing ethically questionable things to keep everyone looking guilty. The real killer is usually fairly obvious midway through. It's really about the protagonist's journey rather than a complex web of lies and secrets. 

Rian Johnson intentionally modelled the Knives Out movies after Agatha Christie *movie adaptations*, in terms of a star-studded ensemble cast at a single exotic, enclosed location. It's a deliberate superficial resemblance to those movies. Narratively, they're more of a mesh-up of crime thrillers (where a protagonist is caught up in a crime mystery) and classic hardboil detective stories.

Ransom was complete surprise (I failed to notice that the dogs didn't like him, I rooted for him after he offered to help Martha) and Fran saying "You did this" really threw me off. Glass Onion, I was thrown off by Miles Bron with iPad, however it was never on him on screen (I knew Apple doesn't allow bad guys to use their products). And because Blanc said "Miles Bron is not an idiot." Just because you happened to guess the killer, doesn't mean they aren't whodunnits. Even one of the movie posters said "A Rian Johnson whodunnit". Knives Out films are the closest thing to whodunnit anyone has done in ages (if you don't count the mediocre Agatha Christie remakes). I mean they are a little unorthodox (in 1st one was wondering where the hell this is going for a long time), but still whodunits. Or comedic crime dramas, you could call them many things. Or what you called them, sure. But I don't think a whodunnit has to be 1 certain way

video_title: Wake Up Dead Man: A Knives Out Mystery | Official Teaser | Netflix
reply_text:
​@shredd5705if your definition of a whodunit is simply "any character in the story could be the killer", that just about describes every other murder mystery ever written. Bcuz in many murder mysteries, the killer is often a character you've already met before in the story, whether they're explicitly marketed as whodunit or not. 

To me at least, a whodunit story should be just as much about the investigation of each of the who's, what they're hiding and lying about. If it just follows the investigation of the murder, it's just a detective murder mystery. 

But the Knives Out movies aren't really that either. While it's structured like a detective story, but the real story is a crime thriller of a protagonist in peril, caught inside this murder mystery. The stakes are stacked and raised against him/her as the story goes on. It's not entirely a new invention either. WHO FRAMED ROGER RABBIT? also does this. 

What I'm saying is Knives Out films are movies with the aesthetics and setup of a Agatha Christie whodunit, while structurally a more straightforward detective story, while underneath that is a crime thriller with a sympathetic protagonist at the center that we root for and want him/her to prevail at the end. They are all these things, all at once. I'm pretty certain that Rian Johnson would agree with this description. 

It's also what's unique about these movies. They essentially have two climaxes. The first is a cerebral climax when Benoit Blanc pieces everything together and solves the mystery. And then the second is the emotional climax when the protagonist wins over the villain, which provides the emotional catharsis.
